summary: O'Neil responds to Trump's reported vulgar comments about Haiti by refusing to repeat them and offering a pointed contrast. She draws on her personal experience traveling in Haiti to make a moral comparison between Haitian people and the president. The brief post uses restraint and dignity to make its critique more powerful than outrage would. Her refusal to perpetuate the comments while still addressing them is itself a rhetorical choice.
key_points: Deliberately refuses to amplify harmful rhetoric by not repeating the president's words
Uses personal travel experience to provide counter-testimony about Haitian people
Redefines 'wealth' as spiritual richness rather than material or political power
Implies the president is impoverished in ways that matter most
money_quotes: I will not repeat what the president said yesterday about Haiti, because it is too mean spirited to perpetuate.
In my travels in Haiti I met people wealthier in spirit than our poor president.
key_insight: True poverty is spiritual, not material, and refusing to amplify cruelty is itself a form of resistance.
